channel.title: Insert on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Insert on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] C++ std::length_error - map max size exceeded
item.description:
std::length_error - map max size exceeded

std::length_error is thrown when inserting into a std::map would exceed its maximum allowed size.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Excessive insertions
std::map<int, int> m;
for (size_t i = 0; i < SIZE_MAX; i++) {
 m[i] = i; // eventually throws
}

// Cause 2: Huge key count
std::map<std::string, int> m;
m.reserve(m.max_size() + 1); // throws

How to Fix

Fix 1: Monitor size

if (m.size() < m.max_size() - 1) {
 m[key] = value;
}

Fix 2: Use try-catch

try {
 m[key] = value;
} catch (const std::length_error& e) {
 std::cerr << "Map full: " << e.what() << std::endl;
}

Fix 3: Use unordered_map with max_load_factor

std::unordered_map<int, int> m;
m.max_load_factor(0.25); // control growth

Related Errors

  • std::length_error - vector — vector too large.
  • std::length_error - string — string too long.
  • std::bad_alloc — allocation failure.

item.title: [Solution] MyBatis-Plus DataIntegrityViolationException Fix
item.description:
MyBatis-Plus DataIntegrityViolationException Fix

A DataIntegrityViolationException in MyBatis-Plus occurs when a BaseMapper method (insert, update) violates database constraints. This wraps the underlying SQL exception.

What This Error Means

Common message:

  • DataIntegrityViolationException: Duplicate entry for key
  • DataIntegrityViolationException: Column cannot be null

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Duplicate key on insert
UserMapper userMapper = ...;
User user = new User();
user.setEmail("existing@email.com"); // Unique constraint
userMapper.insert(user); // DataIntegrityViolationException

// Cause 2: Null value on NOT NULL column
User user = new User();
user.setName(null); // Column has NOT NULL constraint
userMapper.insert(user);

How to Fix

Fix 1: Check before insert

@Service
public class UserService {

 @Autowired
 private UserMapper userMapper;

 public void createUser(User user) {
 LambdaQueryWrapper<User> wrapper = new LambdaQueryWrapper<>();
 wrapper.eq(User::getEmail, user.getEmail());
 if (userMapper.selectCount(wrapper) > 0) {
 throw new DuplicateEmailException("Email already exists");
 }
 userMapper.insert(user);
 }
}

Fix 2: Handle exception

try {
 userMapper.insert(user);
} catch (DataIntegrityViolationException ex) {
 if (ex.getMessage().contains("Duplicate entry")) {
 throw new BusinessException("Duplicate entry");
 }
 throw ex;
}

Fix 3: Use saveOrUpdate

IService<User> userService = ...;
userService.saveOrUpdate(user); // Insert or update based on ID

Related Errors

  • /languages/java/mybatis/ — BindingException: Invalid bound statement
  • /languages/java/mybatis-dynamic/ — Dynamic SQL binding errors
  • /languages/java/jpa-constraint/ — ConstraintViolationException

item.title: [Solution] SQLite UNIQUE Constraint Failed
item.description:
SQLite UNIQUE Constraint Failed

A UNIQUE constraint failure occurs when an INSERT or UPDATE attempts to add a value that already exists in a column with a UNIQUE constraint or PRIMARY KEY.

Common Causes

  • Attempting to insert a duplicate primary key value
  • A column with a UNIQUE constraint already contains the value
  • Race condition in concurrent inserts without proper locking
  • The application logic does not check for existing records

How to Fix

Use INSERT OR REPLACE

INSERT OR REPLACE INTO users (id, name, email)
VALUES (1, 'Alice', 'alice@example.com');

Use INSERT OR IGNORE to Skip Duplicates

INSERT OR IGNORE INTO users (id, name, email)
VALUES (1, 'Alice', 'alice@example.com');

Use UPSERT (INSERT ON CONFLICT)

INSERT INTO users (id, name, email)
VALUES (1, 'Alice', 'alice@example.com')
ON CONFLICT(id) DO UPDATE SET
 name = excluded.name,
 email = excluded.email;

Check Existing Values Before Insert

-- Check if the value already exists
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE id = 1;
-- If count > 0, use UPDATE instead of INSERT

Add Auto-Increment for Primary Keys

CREATE TABLE users (
 id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
 name TEXT NOT NULL,
 email TEXT UNIQUE
);

Examples

-- Duplicate primary key
INSERT INTO users (id, name) VALUES (1, 'Alice');
INSERT INTO users (id, name) VALUES (1, 'Bob');
-- Error: UNIQUE constraint failed: users.id

-- Duplicate unique email
INSERT INTO users (id, name, email) VALUES (2, 'Bob', 'alice@example.com');
-- Error: UNIQUE constraint failed: users.email

Related Errors

  • Constraint Error — other constraint violations
  • Database Locked — write lock contention

item.title: E11000 duplicate key error collection
item.description:
This error occurs when you try to insert or update a document with a value that already exists in a unique index field. MongoDB enforces uniqueness constraints and rejects the operation.

Common Causes

  • Inserting a document with a _id or unique field that already exists
  • Missing application-level checks for duplicates
  • Race condition where two processes insert the same key simultaneously
  • Unintended unique index on a field that should allow duplicates

How to Fix

  1. Use upsert to insert-or-update:
db.users.updateOne(
 { email: "user@example.com" },
 { $setOnInsert: { name: "Alice" } },
 { upsert: true }
)
  1. Check existing unique indexes:
db.collection.getIndexes()
  1. Remove an unintended unique index if needed:
db.collection.dropIndex("fieldname_1")
  1. Wrap inserts in a try-catch and handle duplicates gracefully:
try {
 await db.users.insertOne({ _id: "user123", name: "Alice" });
} catch (e) {
 if (e.code === 11000) {
 console.log("Duplicate key, skipping insert");
 } else {
 throw e;
 }
}

Examples

// First insert succeeds
db.users.insertOne({ _id: "user1", name: "Alice" });

// Second insert fails with E11000
db.users.insertOne({ _id: "user1", name: "Bob" });
// E11000 duplicate key error collection: mydb.users dup key: { _id: "user1" }

Related Errors

  • Connection Refused

item.title: MongoDB - E11000 duplicate key error
item.description:
An E11000 duplicate key error occurs when MongoDB attempts to insert or update a document that violates a unique index constraint. The error includes the collection name, index name, and the duplicate key value.

Common Causes

  • Inserting a document with a value that already exists in a unique field
  • Race condition with concurrent inserts on the same unique key
  • Unique index on _id field with manual ID assignment
  • Bulk insert with duplicate values in the batch
  • Missing unique index causing unexpected duplicates

How to Fix

  1. Use insertOne with error handling for duplicates:
try {
 await db.collection('users').insertOne({ email: 'user@example.com' });
} catch (error) {
 if (error.code === 11000) {
 console.log('Duplicate key error:', error.keyValue);
 }
}
  1. Use upsert to handle duplicates gracefully:
await db.collection('users').updateOne(
 { email: 'user@example.com' },
 { $set: { name: 'John', updatedAt: new Date() } },
 { upsert: true }
);
  1. Check existing unique indexes:
const indexes = await db.collection('users').indexes();
console.log(indexes.filter(i => i.unique));
  1. Remove or relax conflicting unique indexes:
await db.collection('users').dropIndex('email_1');
  1. Use bulkWrite with ordered operations and handle duplicates:
const ops = documents.map(doc => ({
 updateOne: {
 filter: { email: doc.email },
 update: { $set: doc },
 upsert: true,
 }
}));
await db.collection('users').bulkWrite(ops, { ordered: false });

Examples

// Error: E11000 duplicate key error collection: mydb.users index: email_1 dup key: { email: "test@example.com" }
await db.collection('users').insertOne({ email: 'test@example.com' });
// If email index is unique and test@example.com already exists

// Fix: handle the duplicate case
try {
 await db.collection('users').insertOne({ email: 'test@example.com' });
} catch (err) {
 if (err.code === 11000) {
 console.log('User already exists');
 }
}

Related Errors

  • Write error
  • Index error

item.title: MongoDB Write Error
item.description:
MongoDB Write Error

A MongoDB write error occurs when a write operation (insert, update, delete) fails. This can be caused by write concern issues, document validation failures, or write conflicts.

Common Causes

  • Write concern timeout exceeded
  • Document validation rules violated
  • Write conflict in multi-statement transactions
  • Disk space exhaustion

How to Fix

Check Write Concern

db.collection.insertOne(
 { name: 'test' },
 { writeConcern: { w: 'majority', wtimeout: 5000 } }
);

Validate Document Schema

db.createCollection('users', {
 validator: {
 $jsonSchema: {
 bsonType: 'object',
 required: ['name', 'email'],
 properties: {
 name: { bsonType: 'string' },
 email: { bsonType: 'string' }
 }
 }
 }
});

Handle Write Conflicts

const session = client.startSession();
try {
 session.startTransaction();
 await collection.updateOne({ _id: 1 }, { $inc: { count: 1 } }, { session });
 await session.commitTransaction();
} catch (e) {
 await session.abortTransaction();
}

Check Disk Space

df -h /var/lib/mongodb

Reduce Write Concern for Performance

db.collection.insertOne(
 { name: 'test' },
 { writeConcern: { w: 1 } } // Acknowledge from primary only
);

Examples

// Write concern timeout
MongoServerError: Not enough data-bearing nodes (5ms timeout)

// Document validation
MongoServerError: Document failed validation

Related Errors

  • Duplicate Key Error — duplicate key violation
  • Timeout Error — operation timeout

item.title: Oracle - ORA-14400: inserted partition key does not map
item.description:
ORA-14400: inserted partition key does not map to any partition occurs when an INSERT statement tries to add a row with a partition key value that does not fall into any defined partition. This is common with range-partitioned tables when new date ranges are needed.

Common Causes

  • Partition range does not cover the inserted date/value
  • Missing partition for the current time period
  • MAXVALUE partition not defined
  • Monthly/weekly partitions not created for future dates
  • Incorrect partition key value

How to Fix

  1. Check existing partitions:
SELECT partition_name, high_value
FROM user_tab_partitions
WHERE table_name = 'SALES'
ORDER BY partition_position;
  1. Add a new partition for the missing range:
ALTER TABLE sales ADD PARTITION p_2024_01
VALUES LESS THAN (TO_DATE('2024-02-01', 'YYYY-MM-DD'));
  1. Add a catch-all MAXVALUE partition:
ALTER TABLE sales ADD PARTITION p_future VALUES LESS THAN (MAXVALUE);
  1. Use interval partitioning for automatic partition creation:
CREATE TABLE sales (
 sale_id NUMBER,
 sale_date DATE,
 amount NUMBER
)
PARTITION BY RANGE (sale_date)
INTERVAL (NUMTOYMINTERVAL(1, 'MONTH'))
(
 PARTITION p_initial VALUES LESS THAN (TO_DATE('2024-01-01', 'YYYY-MM-DD'))
);
  1. Split an existing partition to accommodate new values:
ALTER TABLE sales SPLIT PARTITION p_future
AT (TO_DATE('2024-06-01', 'YYYY-MM-DD'))
INTO (PARTITION p_2024_h1, PARTITION p_future);
  1. Use DBMS_SCHEDULER to create future partitions automatically:
BEGIN
 DBMS_SCHEDULER.CREATE_JOB(
 job_name => 'CREATE_MONTHLY_PARTITIONS',
 job_type => 'PLSQL_BLOCK',
 job_action => 'BEGIN create_next_partition(); END;',
 start_date => SYSTIMESTAMP,
 repeat_interval => 'FREQ=MONTHLY;BYMONTHDAY=28',
 enabled => TRUE
 );
END;

Examples

-- Error: ORA-14400: inserted partition key does not map to any partition
INSERT INTO sales VALUES (1, TO_DATE('2024-03-15', 'YYYY-MM-DD'), 1000);
-- ORA-14400: inserted partition key does not map to any partition

-- Fix: add partition for March 2024
ALTER TABLE sales ADD PARTITION p_2024_03
VALUES LESS THAN (TO_DATE('2024-04-01', 'YYYY-MM-DD'));

INSERT INTO sales VALUES (1, TO_DATE('2024-03-15', 'YYYY-MM-DD'), 1000);
-- 1 row created.

Related Errors

  • Tablespace error
  • Lock error

item.title: SQLite - constraint violation (UNIQUE)
item.description:
SQLite constraint violation error occurs when an INSERT or UPDATE operation violates a table constraint, most commonly a UNIQUE constraint. SQLite enforces constraints strictly and aborts the operation when a violation occurs.

Common Causes

  • INSERT with a duplicate PRIMARY KEY or UNIQUE column value
  • UPDATE that would create duplicate values in a unique column
  • CHECK constraint evaluating to false
  • NOT NULL constraint violated with NULL value
  • FOREIGN KEY constraint violated

How to Fix

  1. Use INSERT OR REPLACE to handle duplicates:
INSERT OR REPLACE INTO users (id, name, email)
VALUES (1, 'John', 'john@example.com');
  1. Use INSERT OR IGNORE to skip duplicates silently:
INSERT OR IGNORE INTO users (id, name, email)
VALUES (1, 'John', 'john@example.com');
  1. Use UPSERT (INSERT … ON CONFLICT) for advanced handling:
INSERT INTO users (id, name, email)
VALUES (1, 'John', 'john@example.com')
ON CONFLICT(id) DO UPDATE SET
 name = excluded.name,
 email = excluded.email;
  1. Check for existing records before inserting:
def safe_insert(conn, user):
 existing = conn.execute(
 "SELECT id FROM users WHERE email = ?", (user['email'],)
 ).fetchone()
 if existing:
 conn.execute(
 "UPDATE users SET name = ? WHERE id = ?",
 (user['name'], existing[0])
 )
 else:
 conn.execute(
 "INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES (?, ?)",
 (user['name'], user['email'])
 )
 conn.commit()
  1. Disable foreign keys temporarily if needed:
PRAGMA foreign_keys = OFF;
-- perform operations
PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON;

Examples

-- Error: UNIQUE constraint failed: users.email
INSERT INTO users (id, name, email) VALUES (1, 'John', 'john@example.com');
-- If email 'john@example.com' already exists

-- Fix: use UPSERT
INSERT INTO users (id, name, email)
VALUES (1, 'John', 'john@example.com')
ON CONFLICT(email) DO UPDATE SET name = 'John';

Related Errors

  • Database locked
  • Syntax error
